speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been going to the bathroom a lot lately—like, every hour or so—and I’m really thirsty all the time.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for coming in. You mentioned frequent urination—how long has that been going on? Any other symptoms?

speaker: patient
text: It started about three days ago. I’ve also felt unusually tired and a bit foggy-headed.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your blood glucose right now. Okay—your reading is 15.0 mmol/L. That’s significantly elevated.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood pressure today is 142 over 88, so we’ll continue monitoring for essential hypertension, which we’ve previously diagnosed.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure is 88 mmHg—still on the higher side, though systolic improved slightly from last visit.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Atorvastatin 40mg at night for cholesterol management—still taking that as prescribed?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, every night. But I’ve had a low-grade fever since yesterday—I took my temperature this morning and it was 38.6 °C.

speaker: doctor
text: I see—you’re running a fever. Let me take your vitals again. Your temperature is indeed 38.6 °C.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 99 bpm—elevated, likely due to the fever and possible metabolic stress.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed some mild chest tightness when walking up stairs—nothing severe, but new for me.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll need to rule out infection or cardiac strain. Let’s repeat your heart rate in a few minutes after you’ve rested.

speaker: doctor
text: Now your heart rate is down to 70 bpm—much more stable, which suggests the earlier elevation was likely reactive to the fever.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your persistently high blood glucose and new symptoms, I’m going to start you on Metformin 1000mg twice daily to help with glycemic control.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that today. Should I still monitor my sugar at home?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—please do. In fact, your most recent fingerstick just now showed 16.0 mmol/L, so we’ll need to reassess dosing in 48 hours if it stays above 13.

speaker: patient
text: That’s strange—I tested before coming in and got 15.0, same as your first reading. I used the same meter.

speaker: doctor
text: Good—consistency helps. We’ll use that 15.0 as our baseline reference point for tracking trends.

speaker: patient
text: I rechecked just now while waiting—still 15.0 mmol/L. So it’s holding steady, at least for the moment.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful—thanks for confirming that number. We’ll keep close watch and adjust Metformin if needed.
